title: Procedure explanation — MRI for a claustrophobic patient
candidateInstructions: You are an FY2 in neurology outpatients. Mrs Sandra Blake, a 48-year-old woman, has been referred for an MRI brain to investigate recurrent headaches. She has told reception she is severely claustrophobic. She is considering refusing the scan. Please counsel her.
examinerNotes: MRI in claustrophobic patient. Candidate must explain: what MRI involves (noisy, enclosed tunnel, 30-60 min), why it's needed, options for claustrophobia (open MRI, pre-medication with short-acting benzodiazepine, patient communication button, headphones). Candidate should NOT minimise the patient's concern.

patient.name: Sandra Blake
patient.presentingComplaint: Needs MRI brain but has severe claustrophobia
patient.reasonForAttendance: I've been told I need a brain scan but I know I will not be able to cope in one of those tunnel machines. I'm going to cancel it.

hpi.character: Recurrent unilateral headaches with visual aura
hpi.severity: Severe during attacks — bedridden for 4-6 hours

patient.emotionalState: Anxious and already decided to cancel. Needs to be convinced without feeling pressured. History of panic attacks in enclosed spaces.
patient.communicationStyle: Determined initially but open to reasonable solutions. Will ask 'is there any other way?' and 'what will happen if I don't have the scan?'
hiddenConcerns: I had a panic attack in a lift last year — this is my worst fear
What if I can't breathe in there?
Can't I have a CT scan instead?
doNotVolunteer: Panic attack history — only if asked about previous experiences in confined spaces
Previous anxiety episodes — only if asked

primaryDiagnosis: Migraines with aura requiring MRI brain — claustrophobia management needed

dataGathering.criterion: Explores nature and severity of claustrophobia
dataGathering.detailedExpectation: Asks about previous experiences, what exactly triggers panic

dataGathering.criterion: Explains why MRI is needed over CT
dataGathering.detailedExpectation: MRI provides better soft tissue detail, no radiation, superior for brain imaging

clinicalManagement.detailedExpectation: Tunnel scanner, very loud noise, 30-60 minutes, need to stay still
Short-acting benzodiazepine (lorazepam or diazepam) before scan with escort
Panic button/buzzer, headphones/music, keeping eyes closed, radiographer talking throughout
Open MRI scanner less claustrophobic — may be available

interpersonalSkills.criterion: Validates and does not dismiss claustrophobia
interpersonalSkills.detailedExpectation: Takes concern seriously — does not say 'it's not that bad'
Does not coerce — explains consequences without pressure

references.source: RCR: MRI in claustrophobic patients — guidance

learningObjectives: Counsel a claustrophobic patient about MRI
